name: Penanggalan
alias: the detaching head · cousin to the Thai Krasue
body: By day she is an ordinary woman, often a midwife, sometimes a village beauty who once bargained for the face she wears. Nothing marks her out; she draws water, tends a fire, greets her neighbours by name and knows the names of their children before the children are born. If anything betrays her it is the faintest sourness about her, a smell of vinegar that clings under the soap and the cooking smoke, easy to mistake for a clean kitchen. The horror is precisely this ordinariness, that she walks among the living as one of them, sits at their bedsides, handles their newborns with hands that have done it a hundred times, and only after dark does the body she wears come apart.

After dark her head lifts free of her shoulders and rises into the black, dragging her stomach and entrails beneath it like a lantern strung from wet rope, the whole of it glowing faintly as it drifts above the rooftops. She hunts the blood of women in labour and of the newly born, settling on the thatch above a birthing room to drink through seams no one can see, and the lore holds that a bomoh can call such a thing into being using the blood of a dead mother. Before dawn she comes home to her sleeping flesh and soaks the trailing organs in a tub of vinegar so they shrink enough to fold back inside her, and it is in that quiet, vulnerable hour, the head returning to the empty neck, that she can be undone. The old origin is plainer still: a woman startled at her vinegar bath, jerking her head up so hard it tore loose and dragged everything after it, flying off in a rage as the first of her kind.

What lingers is the doubling: the thought that the calm woman who delivered the child and the wet light passing over the roof are the same person, and that you will go on greeting her in daylight as though nothing crawls beneath her skin. The suspicion fixes itself, in the older kampongs, to midwives who keep too much to themselves, and to households where a new mother is watched a little too closely. So the women guard their own: scissors laid under the pillow of the one who is due, the thorned leaves of mengkuang and pineapple crossed over the windows so the trailing guts will snag and hold her until the light finds her, and to this day a clump of pandan or jeruju planted close beneath the sill of the room where a woman waits to give birth.
note: The classic defence is botanical. Households lay the thorny leaves of the mengkuang or the jeruju across the windows so the trailing entrails snag and tear; held fast, she cannot withdraw before light finds her. Scatter glass or thorns across the roof and she has nowhere to land at all. Some tellings add a crueller stroke: find her hollow sleeping body and fill the empty neck with broken glass, and when the head returns it cannot reattach.

She is spoken of across the Malay world, in peninsular Malaysia, in Indonesia, and in the Thai south, where a near-identical creature, the Krasue, drifts the night over the rice fields. The version told most often is a cautionary one, aimed at women who would trade too much for beauty or who practise the wrong kind of medicine, and the midwife framing gives the tale its particular dread, the healer at the bedside who is also the thing on the roof.

recountings.text: In the same first-person Fiksyen Shasha account, the writer known as RC relays a cousin's disturbing experience in which a houseguest at their rural village home was eventually revealed to be a penanggal, the detached flying head. The narrator also reports headless, long-haired figures lingering near the bathroom of the home. The author labels the piece as non-fiction, presenting it as a claimed family experience rather than fiction.

In a 2012 Yahoo Malaysia piece, journalist Susan Tam gathered the common beliefs around the hantu penanggalan, noting she is said to pass for an ordinary woman by day and is often imagined disguised as a midwife so she can get close to newborns. A University of Malaya scholar explained the lore that a bomoh could conjure such a spirit using a dead mother's blood. Villagers reportedly knew her by a tell-tale vinegar smell and guarded new mothers with scissors and thorny mengkuang and pineapple leaves. This is reported folklore rather than a single named eyewitness account.
recountings.attribution: Yahoo Malaysia News, reporting by Susan Tam

recountings.text: The most widely circulated origin tale, recorded in Malay folklore studies and summarized on Wikipedia, holds that a woman was soaking in a tub of vinegar in deep concentration when a man burst in and startled her. She jerked her head up so violently that it tore free of her body, dragging her organs and entrails out through the neck, and she flew off in a rage as the first penanggalan. This is canonical lore passed down through generations, not a personal sighting.
recountings.attribution: Widely circulated Malay folklore (origin tale on Wikipedia)

references.title: Penanggalan: Malay Folklore's Floating Vampire Head
Penanggalan: The Disembodied Head with Entrails
Malaysian Ghostly Encounters: 10 Spine-Chilling Urban Myths
